task_title: Try to prove strict-threshold lemma for p<1/3 via threshold-family/minimal-counterexample analysis
notes: The continuous part of the suggested route is complete: strict counterexamples reduce to subset-sum boundary deficits. The exact remaining obstruction is the weighted knapsack down-set inequality mu_p({S:w(S)<=p}) <= 1-p for p<1/3 and all weights w_i<=p. Any counterexample must have support at least 6 and occur at a threshold boundary.

task_title: Attack p<=1/3 sufficiency via fractional-cover/minimax theorem for bad down-sets
notes: The minimax part is fully settled, and FC(p) is exactly equivalent to the Bernoulli inequality. The theorem is proved for reciprocal p=1/k and for structurally important pairwise-heavy/small-support cases. The main remaining task is a two-threshold induction invariant, either in probabilistic form for F_p(t)=inf Pr[weighted sum>=t] or in section form for gluing covers of G_0 and G_1.

task_title: Prove the exact two-threshold knapsack/gluing inequality for the fractional-cover induction
notes: The section separation reduction is verified. The target two-threshold inequality is exactly equivalent, by adding one Bernoulli coordinate and normalizing, to the original one-threshold Bernoulli tail problem. Thus it is not an easier auxiliary lemma, though it is the right gluing form. It is proved here for reciprocal p, augmented support at most 5, pairwise-heavy augmented weights, and a Cantelli variance-slack regime. The remaining task is the full subthreshold lemma Pr_p[sum w_i v_i > tau] >= p for tau < p <= 1/3, equivalently the original inequality.

task_title: Moment and polynomial-dual attack on p<1/3 sufficiency
notes: The full p<1/3 sufficiency is not closed here. The main new rigorous progress is a family of polynomial-dual certificates, a Berry-Esseen diffuse case, and a complete strict proof for support size 6, pushing any strict counterexample beyond the previously handled support sizes to support at least 7.

task_title: Search for degree 5/6 polynomial or moment certificates valid on the base interval [1/6,1/3].
notes: The full p in [1/6,1/3] moment proof is not closed here. The main new tools are exact mu_5 and mu_6 formulas, degree-6 square-complement certificates, an exact degree-6 SDP formulation, and a support-7 proof for p<=1/4. Remaining hard cases are support at least 7 with p>1/4 and moment vectors that are neither Berry-Esseen diffuse nor caught by the explicit degree-6 certificates.

task_title: Exploit the bootstrap reduction and attack the base interval [1/6,1/3] by combinatorial and minimal-counterexample methods.
notes: The full base interval [1/6,1/3] is not closed here. New progress rules out support 7 up to p=1/4, support 8 up to p=1/5, and all support-7 upper-interval cases except those with three disjoint losing pairs. The most promising remaining route is to combine the degree-sequence test with shifted/threshold-graph structure to prove the needed tradeoff between losing pair density and higher losing layers.

task_title: Develop a Berry-Esseen plus critical-index proof for large support.
notes: The full base interval [1/6,1/3] is not completely closed here. The main progress is an explicit finite-head/regular-tail certificate, a small-head corollary with constants, a one-dominant-atom refinement, a critical-index decay and tail-mass reduction for nonregular tails, a deterministic tail-filling finite reduction, and a new support-9 proof in the lowest base range. The remaining hard case is a large geometrically decaying head or a regular tail that first appears only after a large head, where finite-head layer-counting or relative tail estimates are still needed.

task_title: Attack the minimal-counterexample merge obstruction.
notes: The support-7 upper interval is closed for strict p<1/3, so combined with prior work support 7 is eliminated across the base interval except for the non-strict endpoint p=1/3, which is handled by the cyclic reciprocal argument. The arbitrary-support merge contradiction is not yet proved; the strongest structural output is that a minimal counterexample must force every mergeable pair's lower residual window to have unusually poor p-biased losing-shadow expansion.

task_title: Close the next finite-support obstruction, especially support 8.
notes: Support 8 is now eliminated across the full base interval [1/6,1/3) when combined with the earlier [1/6,1/5] result. The proof relies on layer-counting plus several tradeoffs: Kruskal–Katona in the lower range, 2-intersecting complement bounds for high layers, Kneser supersaturation for losing triples, and a high-pair refinement in [1/4,2/7]. Support 9 remains known only on [1/6,1/5] from the earlier work.

task_title: Exploit the complement high-bias formulation and k-wise intersecting LTF structure.
notes: The target theorem has been reduced to the original weighted lower-tail inequality because k-wise intersection is automatic. A sharp structural theorem was proved for support excess at most 2, giving the full result for support size <=k+2 and in particular for k=3 up to five positive weights. The first remaining obstruction is the k=3,n=6 rank-three case, where one needs a weighted enumeration of shifted bad pairs and triples satisfying e2+(p/q)e3 <= D(p).

task_title: Close support 9 or produce robust finite-head certificates for the critical-index route.
notes: Support 9 is now eliminated on the full range [1/6,1/4] when combined with the earlier [1/6,1/5] result. The remaining interval [1/4,1/3) is reduced to a concrete D4 structural problem: one needs to rule out very large losing 4-layers when the winning-pair graph has only a few edges, especially near p=2/7. The deficiency cascade and the exact 8-head two-threshold formulation look like the most useful next tools.

task_title: Attack the complement high-bias LTF theorem beyond the Frankl-Tokushige endpoint.
notes: The main new progress is a complete support-size <=k+3 theorem, which settles the previously open k=3,n=6 obstruction and pushes any first high-bias counterexample to at least 7 positive weights. The remaining k=3,n=7 case has rank at most 4, but unlike n=6 it allows bad pairs disjoint from bad triples, so the 2-intersecting triple argument no longer applies directly.

task_title: Attack the k=3 complement high-bias LTF theorem beyond the Frankl-Tokushige endpoint.
notes: The k=3 high-bias LTF theorem is now proved through support 7, improving the previous support <= 6 barrier. The first possible strict counterexample must have at least 8 positive weights. The next obstruction is support 8, where rank can reach 5 and the clean n=7 complement-of-bad-triple good-pair argument loses a dimension.

task_title: Close or sharply reduce strict reduced support 9 for p in [1/4,1/3).
notes: The remaining support-9 upper interval is closed in the strict-reduced setting. The decisive new input is that D4 is not merely intersecting: it is an intersecting family inside the independent 4-sets of the winning-pair threshold graph. The supplied exact enumeration plus LP/Sturm certificate is the finite certificate for all m and all r in [1/3,1/2).

task_title: Turn support-9 finite certificates into a robust finite-head/tail or critical-index proof.
notes: The direct h=9 deterministic tail-filling conjecture is false. The useful replacement is a smoothed finite-head certificate using the lower convex envelope of a Gaussian tail, combined with critical-index decay to bound the nonregular buffer after the first nine weights. The remaining work is to verify the resulting compact finite-dimensional certificate, or to supplement it with sharper relative/moderate-deviation estimates in the small-variance regimes.

task_title: Attack the arbitrary-support k=3 high-bias LTF theorem.
notes: The theorem is reduced to two sharp structural obstructions beyond the known support <=9 certificate: any counterexample must be strongly non-Gaussian in L3/L2 effective dimension and must be merge-unstable, exhibiting adjacent-slab mass inversion for the two smallest weights. Proving the adjacent-slab monotonicity/stability inequality would close the full k=3 interval by repeated merging to support 9.

task_title: Turn the smoothed 9-head critical-index certificate into explicit inequalities or a proof.
notes: The t15 lower bound is correct as a sufficient certificate, and it proves useful analytic cases such as alpha+gamma<=sigma. However, the hoped-for universal first-9-head finite-dimensional inequality is false. To continue this route one must add extra tail information: buffer variance or exact finite buffer layers for the nonregular part, and relative large-deviation or tail-ratio estimates for the regular remainder.

task_title: Salvage pair merging by proving a stability or averaged good-merge theorem for minimal counterexamples.
notes: The exact stability dichotomy is reduced to ruling out a small-gap configuration with at least nine positive bottom-pair level-2 Fourier coefficients of size >=epsilon and strong pivotal covariance constraints. A stronger local statement is false: all bottom-5 pairs can be bad in support 10, but the explicit obstruction is safely non-counterexample with Pr(D)<q.

task_title: Attack the asymmetric one-coordinate tail-ratio inequality that could replace absolute Berry-Esseen.
notes: The lemma is decisive but not weaker than the original theorem: proving it for all p<=1/3 would prove the full result. It is fully proved at p=1/k, for large tail atoms, for tail support <=3, and under an explicit regular-tail Berry-Esseen margin. The remaining target is the arbitrary-support non-reciprocal range, equivalently a robust finite subset-counting inequality for rational p=m/N.

task_title: Use the quantitative merge/Fourier constraints to rule out minimum-support counterexamples with support at least 10.
notes: The remaining obstruction is now a tiny-gap complete-positive-level-2 LTF: every pair merge must be bad, every weight is at least epsilon/p, and for support 10 the counterexample gap would have to be below about 0.0102. A full contradiction would follow by ruling out complete pair-positivity for mean-threshold LTFs in this range or by proving a support-9 merge-stability margin at this scale.
